summary: Asparouhova critiques voting as a governance mechanism, arguing it was designed for 18th-century conditions that no longer apply. She identifies three core flaws: voting can never be truly representative (especially in decentralized communities where the total population is unknown), it promotes zero-sum competition rather than cooperation, and it creates a veneer of objectivity that absolves people of the hard work of compromise. Drawing on cooperative board games and modern open source governance models like lazy consensus, she proposes cooperative mechanics as an alternative framework. She suggests systems with enforced deadlines, auto-renewals, expiration dates, and fail-deadlies that incentivize action and collaboration rather than opposition. The post frames our current era as a 'stag hunt' where cooperative governance is both possible and necessary.
key_points: Voting was designed for small, homogeneous populations in the 1700s and doesn't scale to modern decentralized communities where the total population is unknown
Voting promotes zero-sum competition and faction formation rather than cooperation, and the U.S. founders failed to anticipate political parties spanning across branches of government
On-chain governance and over-engineered systems create a 'veneer of objectivity' that lets people avoid the hard work of disagreement and compromise

Cooperative governance models like lazy consensus (used in Node.js and Rust communities) flip incentives so objecting is expensive and inaction is the default, rewarding reputation over mere participation
Reputation is becoming more powerful than money as a social currency, and cooperative systems can leverage this more democratically than voting systems
money_quotes: If we're coming up with wild ideas for social coordination, voting seems like an awfully familiar solution.

Over-engineered systems create a 'veneer of objectivity' that lets us put blind faith into something greater than ourselves — and in doing so, absolve ourselves of the very real, hard work of disagreeing and compromising with our fellow humans.
If we ever get to the point where we blindly accept what a bunch of code tells us to do, machines will have won and humanity, in all its creative chaos, will sink into decline.
Under voting, it's cheap to veto (i.e. vote against something), which has led to an anti-pattern of outrage today. Not only is it cheap to disagree, but you're often rewarded for it.
Cooperative mechanics flip this around, so it's cheap to do nothing and expensive to raise an objection. That means people reserve their objections for stuff they really care about.
It's impossible to maintain deep knowledge of every political topic of importance. Cooperative governance gives us permission to be curators, focusing on just our areas of passion and expertise.
Votes can be manipulated by propaganda and special interests. Reputation takes time to build, but it belongs to you.
Voting wasn't designed for scale, and it's not going to carry us through the next era of governance.
key_insight: Voting is an 18th-century solution poorly suited to modern decentralized governance; cooperative mechanics that make objection expensive and default to action offer a more scalable alternative.
